coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multicolored photoluminescent carbon nanodots and applications to secured tags in anti-counterfeiting, traceability and digital passport product

coredata.dc:description: With the objective of achieving better product traceability, generating digital passport product and combating counterfeiting, we demonstrate a fully bio-sourced approach featuring photoluminescent carbon nanodot-based tags. A straightforward synthesis procedure is first developed, whereby multicolored photoluminescent colloidal carbon nanodots are obtained from coffee grounds through a process of carbonization and solid-liquid extraction. Carbon-based nanoparticles are indeed renowned for their minimal cytotoxicity, photochemical stability, environmental durability and ease of surface functionalization. A versatile tagging process is then proposed, whereby a drop of synthesized carbon nanodot-based bio-resist is directly deposited onto the surface to be marked. The surface is then structured into the shape of patterns of interest by means of a technique derive from UV microprinting. In order to enhance security and tracking capabilities, the obtained marking patterns can incorporate supplementary coded information, such as alphanumeric codes, QR codes, barcodes, and so forth. The mechanical and optical robustness of the obtained bio-sourced secured tags is demonstrated through various concrete use cases, which illustrate their suitability for use as both graphic identification elements and serializable data elements, particularly in the aerospace sector.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Developed synthesis route for making multicolored CNDs from coffee grounds.
(a) Enlarged FT-IR spectra, (b) XRD patterns and (c) Raman spectra of CNDs-W CNDs-E and CNDs-D.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) photoluminescence emission spectra of CNDs. Optical images of CNDs-W, CNDs-E and CNDs-D under UV@365 nm irradiation and corresponding PLQY are inserted. (c) Mappings of the emission wavelength as a function of the excitation wavelength in the case of CNDs-W, CNDs-E and CNDs-D.

(a) Direct UV microprinting process of bio-nanocomposite to create bio-tags. (b) Optical microscopy images of bio-tags in the shape of five square patterns with decreasing dimensions. Such patterns were fabricated on a glass surface in both groove or pit like configurations using CNDs-E bio-nanocomposite. (c) Optical microscopy zoom and associated PL spectra obtained on the five square patterns under various light configurations: (1) bright field mode and no emission filter, (2) UV/blue excitation at 365 nm and no emission filter, (3) UV/blue excitation at 365 nm and emission filtered after 385 nm, and (4) UV/blue excitation at 381 nm and emission filtered after 520 nm. The corresponding PL spectra are plotted.

Bio-nanocomposite-based tags applied to various use cases: (a) a banana, (b) a jewelry, and (c) an aircraft blade, (d) paper and (e) the surface of an airbag. They are observed in various conditions: without UV excitation, under UV/blue excitation, under an optical microscope, using a smartphone. In the later conditions, for the first (a), (b) and (c) the information of the tag if exposed to UV can be decoded by a dedicated app.
Elemental analysis, zeta potential and average particle size of CND-W, CND-E and CND-D.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multicolored photoluminescent carbon nanodots and applications to secured tags in anti-counterfeiting, traceability and digital passport product

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the objective of achieving better product traceability, generating digital passport product and combating counterfeiting, we demonstrate a fully bio-sourced approach featuring photoluminescent carbon nanodot-based tags. A straightforward synthesis procedure is first developed, whereby multicolored photoluminescent colloidal carbon nanodots are obtained from coffee grounds through a process of carbonization and solid-liquid extraction. Carbon-based nanoparticles are indeed renowned for their minimal cytotoxicity, photochemical stability, environmental durability and ease of surface functionalization. A versatile tagging process is then proposed, whereby a drop of synthesized carbon nanodot-based bio-resist is directly deposited onto the surface to be marked. The surface is then structured into the shape of patterns of interest by means of a technique derive from UV microprinting. In order to enhance security and tracking capabilities, the obtained marking patterns can incorporate supplementary coded information, such as alphanumeric codes, QR codes, barcodes, and so forth. The mechanical and optical robustness of the obtained bio-sourced secured tags is demonstrated through various concrete use cases, which illustrate their suitability for use as both graphic identification elements and serializable data elements, particularly in the aerospace sector.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The prevalence of counterfeiting and piracy represents a significant concern in numerous countries and across a large range of sectors. Over the past two decades, these operations represent an annual multi-billion-dollar of illicit sector that exerts a negative influence on the global economy [1]. Consequently, any system designed to combat counterfeiting, track and preserve information must be based on a secure authentication mechanism [2]. On top of that, with the need of more sustainability, transparency and traceability in industry practices, intense ongoing discussions led to introduction and development of Digital Product Passport (DPP) solutions [3]. In particular, it was recently officially presented and approved in circular economy action plan of European Union [3]. Consequently, numerous tracking and anti-counterfeiting approaches have been the subject of investigation since a permanent renewal is required to maintain a head start on forgers [4–8].

Among them, photoluminescent nanomaterials have successfully been applied as taggants benefiting from their unique optical properties [9–12]. While cadmium-based quantum dots have demonstrated improved performances in terms of quantum yield, photostability, tunability of emission [13–15], these materials are toxic, classified as Carcinogenic, Mutagenic and Reprotoxic substances (CMRs) which might limit their usage in concrete everyday applications. In light of these considerations, efforts have been dedicated to the synthesis of sustainable and reliable photoluminescent nanomaterials, in particular promising carbon dots (CDs) ones [16–22]. Carbon dots not only overcome the disadvantages of photobleaching and instability, but also offer the advantages of low toxicity, high stability, good biocompatibility and abundant functional groups. Consequently, they have been employed not only as anti-counterfeiting solutions [17] but also in bio-imaging devices, optoelectronics, detection, etc. [17,21,23]. In particular, CDs have been successfully introduced in secure (food) packaging as recently reviewed [24,25]. In order to add various levels of security and code information at the milli, micro or nano scale, CD dispersions have been combined to patterning techniques such as inkjet printing [26,27], photolithography [27,28] and 3D printing [29]. However, emphasises need to be pursed in order to address demanding applications with focus on more robust, stable and scalable CD synthesis coupled to versatile green patterning methods featuring unclonable functions. In particular, the adhesion on various types of surfaces (in terms of dimensions, curvature and nature) has to be addressed without altering the tagged product/document itself along with deeper investigation on long-term aging in harsh environmental conditions, both criteria being mandatory to valid for user cases applications but not really discussed in literature. In particular, selected solutions should ban every solvent-based and not chemically resistant solutions.

To tackle those challenges, the presented work highlights the development of a safer synthesis route to produce bio-sourced carbon nanodots (CNDs) from coffee grounds. The structural properties of these CNDs are fully characterized along with the performances of their optical properties. In the interest of ensuring the authentication and traceability of products, we demonstrate a direct UV microprinting marking method employing a bio-nanocomposite obtained by mixing a UV-sensitive bio-sourced resist with as-synthesized CNDs. The aging and robustness of obtained markers are tested. As proof of concept, they are applied to a number of demanding practical applications.

The waste coffee grounds (Lavazza, 100 % Arabica) were obtained from the lab coffee shop machine. The ethanol, nitric acid (ACS reagent, 70 %) and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, ACS reagent, ≥99.8 %) utilized in this experiment were purchased from Merck. The soy-based bio-resist was procured from ANYCUBIC.

Carbon dots are primarily composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, and they possess numerous chemical groups (e.g., carboxylic acids, alcohols, ketones) on their surfaces [25], which render them soluble in water [19,35,36]. These functional groups modify the optical and chemical properties of CDs, and they can be adapted by controlling the reaction conditions for CD synthesis, such as the solvent, temperature, and pH [37–39]. Furthermore, CDs possess intrinsic photoluminescence properties that can be enhanced through the incorporation of heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, sulfur, or phosphorus, or by passivating their surface [21].

In literature, the synthesis of bio-based CDs is achieved through the use of organic materials as precursors, including coffee grounds, lemon juice, banana juice, black soya beans, tea waste, and others [5,40–43]. A variety of synthesis methods was thus applied [44] using microwave, solvothermal, hydrothermal, pyrolysis, and laser ablation techniques. The aforementioned synthesis methods facilitate the production of CDs or CNDs with markedly disparate properties, thereby underscoring the pivotal role of process selection.

Herein, we propose a low-cost mechanism for the formation of CNDs based on a simpler straightforward synthesis approach that incorporates both pyrolysis and carbonization steps, combined with a solid/liquid extraction step, in which water, ethanol and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) are used as extracting solvents (Fig. 1 ). Initially, 40 g of coffee grounds are dried in an oven at 100 °C for 1 h. After dehydration, the dark-brown powder is annealed at 500 °C for 1 h in a pyrolysis furnace to produce a black powder. As illustrated in the Fig. 1, the color of wasted coffee gets darker as the temperature increased. The black coffee powder is then cooled at room temperature. It is further mixed with 50 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 1 h. A solid/liquid extraction of this mixture is next performed at 100 °C for 1 h. The aqueous solution synthesized is subsequently purified by a membrane filtration, centrifugation and sonication process. Under UV irradiation at 365 nm, the obtained CND solution dispersed in water emits in the blue wavelength region, with a final powder mass of 300 mg. They can therefore be stored in the refrigerator for further use. The preparation of cyan and green emitting CNDs is analogous to that of the previous blue emitting CNDs, with the exception that the extraction solvent was altered from water to ethanol or DMF respectively. Then, during the solid/liquid extraction step, 10 µL of nitric acid is added to each solution (ethanol and DMF) and the temperature for the extraction is 78 °C for synthesis in ethanol and 150 °C for synthesis in DMF.

Compared to other methods [9,30–34], the proposed synthesis approach allows employing a reduced number of identical steps to easily obtain bio-sourced CNDs with respective emission spectra in the blue, cyan, and green regions of the visible spectrum. In addition, it limits the usage of complex equipment and the use of toxic solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of bio-tags by UV microprinting
ce:section.ce:para: To mark a surface by UV microprinting, a FISNAR SL101 N fluid dispenser (pressure = 1.5 bar and deposition duration = 0.1 second) deposits a drop of UV sensitive bio-nanocomposite (m = 0.25 mg) directly onto the surface to be marked. A transparent, structured poly (dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) stamp with a thickness of 6 mm incorporating the desired tag patterns is then manually pressed onto the surface to be marked. Subsequently, the stack is subjected to ultraviolet light (365 nm) using a torch with a power of 2000 mW/cm2 at a working distance of 7.5 cm for a duration of 30 s. Note that if the tags are formed on surfaces which are outside, under daylight conditions, UV insolation by the torch is not required and the photopolymerization occurs in seconds. Following cross-linking, the PDMS stamp is peeled off from the surface, leaving 3D photoluminescent bio-nanocomposite structures as tags on the surface. The same PDMS stamp can be reused at least 30 times and recycled after cleaning by adhesive tape.

To propose a full bio sourced solution, we selected UV-curable epoxy plant-based UV resist as the matrix for our nanocomposite. This bio-resist is created from green, natural soya extracts and has been tested for RoHS and REACh compliance, as well as the EN 71–3:2013 safety standard. It is so biocompatible and eco-friendly. With a transparent color and a reliable viscosity of 150–300 mPa-s at 25 °C, it is sensitive to 355 nm ∼ 410 nm UV light. This bio-resist is initially designed for use in 3D object printing in stereolithography processes. This resist was nominated for four key reasons: (i) its biocompatibility properties, which enable the user to work in an environment free from BPA and benzene, which are harmful chemicals; (ii) its chemical resistance to ageing; (iii) its favorable photoinitiation properties, enabling it to undergo polymerization under solar irradiation in a matter of seconds; and (iv) its resistance to solvents and ease in particle dispersion.

While various solvents have been used in the extracting step of the proposed CND synthesis, it is worth noting that dispersions can be dried to get nanopowders that can be directly mixed with the UV bio-resist to obtain a solvent-free bio-nanocomposite. However, if a better CND homogeneity within the bio-nanocomposite is needed, whatever dried CNDs-W or CNDs-D can be redispersed in ethanol which appears to be the best dispersing media. It indeed maintains the overall bio-nanocomposite structure, its adhesion properties on surfaces, along with its global robustness to ageing and its chemical resistance. By the way, this fabrication is generic so the nanocomposite formulation can be extended to any sort of photoluminescent colloidal nano-objects or nano-powders. In a typical formulation, 5 g of soy-based bio-resist are simply mixed with 250 µL of synthesized CNDs in ethanol. The concentration of the colloidal dispersion is adjusted in advance according to the desired mass percentage of CNDs in the final composite, which is classically between 1 and 5 % of the total mass. The resulting bio-nanocomposite can be kept in an amber vial at ambient temperature for up to a year with no noticeable mechanical deterioration. From 200 mg of bio-nanocomposite, it is possible to fabricate around 800 millimeter-large bio-tags and the fabrication is easily scalable to address mass production.

UV photolithography processes were performed on a maskless Digital Micromicror Device (DMD) based equipment, the SmartPrint UV (SPUV) machine developed by Microlight3D, Fr. The various manufacturing stages involve the use of SU8 3050 resist from MicroResist Technologies, and Sylgard 184 PDMS (polydimethylsiloxane) from Dow. Following the cleaning of the 2-inch silicon wafer, the SU8 3050 photosensitive resist is spin-coated onto the wafer surface. Subsequently, the wafer is subjected to two stages of annealing, for a duration of two minutes at a temperature of 65 °C, followed by an eight-minute annealing period at 100 °C. It is then insulated with a 2.5x objective for 23 s at around 130 mW.cm². After a two-stage post-insulation annealing process, comprising 2 min at 65 °C and 8 min at 100 °C, the resist is developed for 4 min in a SU8 developer solution, thereby revealing the patterns. PDMS stamps are manufactured using a Sublym™ equipment from Eden Tech, with the objective of increasing the time efficiency of the process. The PDMS is cast on to the mold and the assembly placed in the Sublym™ machine, which has been previously preheated to 100 °C for 60 s. The temperature is maintained at 100 °C for a period of 4 min before removing the cured PDMS mold.

The percentage of carbon and nitrogen present in the particles was determined by means of a PerkinElmer 2400 Series II flash combustion analyser. In the reactor, the elements in the samples are transformed into simple gases (CO₂, N₂). The resulting gases are then homogenized and separated on a chromatographic column coupled to a catharometric detector. This is used to determine the carbon and nitrogen content as a function of the thermal conductivity of the gas. The various functional groups in the particles were identified using a Thermo-SCIENTIFIC Nicolet IS50 - ATR diamond FTIR spectrometer. Each spectrum was acquired by performing 32 scans between 4000 and 650 cm-1 with a resolution of 2 cm-1. The absorption properties of the CNDs were quantified using a CARY 5000 UV–VIS-PIR spectrometer in the wavelength range of 200 to 800 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) of the CNDs and of the tags obtained was evaluated using an OLYMPUS MX53 direct microscope in reflection mode. The quantum yield of the CNDs was determined using a Quantaurus-QY Plus spectrometer (UV-NIR absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer) from HAMAMATSU with an excitation source from a 150 W xenon arc lamp with a BT-CCD linear image sensor photo-detector. The PLQY was calculated as the ratio of the area between the photons emitted (emission peak area) to the difference between the areas of the excitation peaks of the reference and the sample, representing the quantity of photons absorbed. The surface potential (ζ) of the CNDs was measured by a DELSA Nano equipment from Beckman Coulter. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 1400) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM was performed on a Cs corrected Hitachi HF3300 microscope (I2TEM) operated at 300 kV) were employed to ascertain the size of the CNDs and to observe the microscopic morphology of the synthesized materials. Since the various synthesis led to polydispersed CDs, distributions were determined by TEM image analysis using a procedure described in Supporting Information. The crystal structure of the CNDs was also established by preparing a powder diagram of the dried particles. A Panalytical Empyrean X-ray diffractometer was employed, utilizing the Kα line of a cobalt anode (λ = 1.789 Å) and two incident slits of 1° and ¼°. Raman spectroscopy of the CNDs was conducted using a Raman spectrometer (XPLORA-HORIBA-JOBIN YVON) with a 785 nm laser. A Profilm3D optical profilometer was used to measure the thickness of the tags.

In the present study, CNDs were prepared through a pyrolysis and carbonization strategy utilizing coffee grounds as a carbon source (see previous experimental section). The intermediate structures present in coffee grounds, including hydrophilic ligands comprising hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and amine groups, are formed during the brewing process and make coffee grounds an optimal carbon source for obtaining multicolored CDs or CNDs [9,45–47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of as synthesized carbon nanodots

ce:section.ce:para: The surface functional groups and chemical structures of CNDs were characterized using Fourier Transform InfraRed spectroscopy (FT-IR) as illustrated in Fig. 2 .a. The FT-IR spectra of carbon nanodots in water (CNDs-W), carbon nanodots in ethanol (CNDs-E) and carbon nanodots in DMF (CNDs-D) exhibit a high degree of similarity (cf. Figure S1 in Supporting Information). The wide peaks observed at 3420 cm-1 correspond to the ν (O–H/N–H) stretching vibrations absorption peak (cf. Figure S1, SI). The absorption peaks at 2946 and 2842 cm-1 are attributed to stretching vibration of ν(C–H) bond, which originates in sp3-hybridized alkane bonds (cf. Figure S1). Peaks at 1734 and 1150 cm-1 seen in Fig. 2 .a are due to existence of C = O and C–O–C, respectively. The bands at 1382 and 1164 cm-1 may be indicative of saturated and unsaturated amines with an aromatic system. Moreover, the FT-IR analysis indicates that the quantity of C = O and C–N bands increases when the particles are synthesized in ethanol or DMF.

Table 1 presents a summary of the results of the elemental analysis, zeta potential and average particle size by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) of CNDs-W, CNDs-E and CNDs-D (see previous experimental section and SI for details). Results of the elementary analysis reveal that, as confirmed by the previous infrared study, the quantity of nitrogen increases in accordance with the synthesis solvent employed, with values of 3, 4 and 7, respectively, observed for CNDs-W, CNDs-E and CNDs-D.

Table 1 also shows that CNDs-W, CNDs-E and CNDs-D exhibit electrophoretic mobility in the range of −1.96E-04 cm2/Vs, which corresponds based on the Smoluchowski model to zeta potential values of −23, −26 and −30 mV, respectively considering an average applied electric field of 30V/cm. It reveals the presence of negatively charged groups on their surfaces which contribute to the electrostatic colloidal stability of dispersions.

Developed synthesis led to polydispersed CNDs and agglomerates with various morphologies (mostly spherical and rod like) and structures (both crystalline and amorphous), and the associated average sizes reported in Table 1 were obtained through TEM analysis (cf. Figure S2, SI). The X-ray diffraction patterns of CNDs-W, CNDs-E and CNDs-D (Fig. 2 .b) show two broad peaks of approximately equal intensity at 25° and 44° when the Kα line of a cobalt anode (λ = 1.789 Å) is utilized. The peaks at 25° correspond to the level of the literature in the (002) plane of the graphite, while those at 44° correspond to the stacking of the intermediate layers of the graphite in the (100) plane [33]. The broad XRD peaks indicate the presence of highly disordered carbon atoms in the CNDs [33,48–50]. The observed differences in the XRD patterns of synthesized CNDs are likely due to the fact that their carbon cores are not pure graphitic carbon, but may contain various sp3 hybrid carbons, as well as functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen [51]. The Raman spectrum displayed in Fig. 2 .c reveals the presence of two peaks at 1368 cm-1 and 1446 cm-1, respectively, in the CNDs-W particles. These peaks are consistent with the cellulose I, cellulose II, and cellulose III allomorphs previously reported by Atalla et al. [52]. and Agarwal [53,54]. In the case of CNDs-E and CNDs-D, a single peak was observed at 1370 cm-1, which is indicative of the carbon sp3 disordering [10,55]. The results reveal that the structure of the obtained carbon nanomaterials tends to become more disordered with the introduction of heteroatoms, which may cause the formation of additional defects, ultimately leading to greater disorder in the materials [33,56]. As a consequence, the observation of structurally distinct functional groups can result in the emergence of different emission wavelengths for CNDs.
The optical properties of as-synthetized CNDs were characterized at room temperature by UV–visible absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 3 .

For CNDs-W, a peak absorption band at 270 nm is observed, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the sp2 aromatic carbons (C = C bonds). The broad peak observed at 350 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition in the double-bonded C = O radical present in the CNDs-W and CNDs-E samples. The CNDs-D exhibit a peak in the visible wavelength region around 434 nm, which may be attributed to the n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structure containing C = N and C = O bonds (Fig. 3 .a). Moreover, the optical band gap (Eg) of CNDs can be estimated from the Tauc plot [αhν]2 vs hν curve) for direct bandgap semiconductors. Here, α, h, and ν represent the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency [57]. From Figure S4 in SI, the energy gaps (Eg) of CNDs-W, CNDs-E, and CNDs-D, are estimated at 3.24 ± 0.1, 2.88 ± 0.1, and 2.03 ± 0.2 eV, respectively. These results demonstrate that CNDs display a quantum size effect contingent on the solvent employed for the solid/liquid extraction process. In addition, we performed extra experiments (cf. Figure S5, SI) to rule out any solvatochromic effect. As the size of the CNDs increases, the band gaps decrease, and the emission maxima shift to longer wavelengths [18]. Fig. 3 b shows that the emission wavelengths for CNDs-W, CNDs-E, and CNDs-D are 470, 532, and 552 nm, respectively, when they are excited at 381 nm. It corresponds to the blue, cyan and green luminescence observed on pictures inserted in Fig. 3 b. The photoluminescence (PL) of CDs or CNDs is generally dependent on the quantum confinement effect and surface states. However, the precise PL mechanism remains unclear [58]. Furthermore, it is established that conventional CDs exhibit a broad band of luminescence, with a half-maximum width (FWHM) exceeding 100 nm [59–62], as illustrated in Fig. 3 .b. The quantum yield of CDs or CNDs is dependent upon the method of synthesis and the type of biomass precursor utilized. It has been observed that the majority of these materials exhibit a quantum yield that ranges between 2 % and 84 % [40–42,63–66]. In this context, the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of CNDs-W, CNDs-E, and CNDs-D were determined to be 9 %, 12 %, and 10 %, respectively.

The presence of heteroatoms, as evidenced by UV-visible and infrared spectra, plays an important role in the emission of long-wave radiation. A number of reports [31,67–69] have proved that the emission characteristics of carbon-based particles are influenced by the presence of various trap states, which are generated by the doping of heteroatoms or by surface functional groups linked to the carbon core [18]. The corresponding emission mechanism is referred to as extrinsic emission. It has been demonstrated that surface defects are predominantly generated by the oxidation of the surface, which acts as an excitonic capture centre, resulting in the emission of the defect state. As the oxidation level of the surface increases, more trap states are introduced into the structure, causing the CD emission to red shift [18]. Herein, the fluorescence emission spectra of the as-synthesized CNDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths, ranging from 360 to 520 nm with a continuous span of 15 nm, as show in Fig. 3.c. When CNDs are extracted with water or ethanol, the emission intensity increases from λexc 360 nm to 420 nm, followed by a progressively decrease down to λexc 525 nm. CNDs-W and CNDs-E have maximum emission intensities of 515 and 526 nm, respectively, at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. It shows the dependency of the emission on excitation [21,70–72]. However, for CNDs-D, there is a notable variation in photoluminescence intensity when excited between λexc 360 to 520 nm, while λem fluctuates just minimally. To summarize, the CNDs exhibit an emission-independent characteristic, whereby the red shift emission peak is not significantly affected. According to Wenbo Zhang et al., as the degree of carbonization increases, the main position of the CD fluorescence emission centre may gradually become the emission of the conjugated carbon core itself [10]. The solvent may also be responsible for this phenomenon. Slower solvent relaxation in polar solvents favours the formation of excitation dependent fluorescence features. Thus, solvent polarity plays an important role too in the excitation dependence of fluorescence, which is also confirmed by Wang et al. and Nandi et al. [73,74]. In light of the results of related research [10,70–74] that has typically employed toxic reagents or solvents, and of our own work, we posit that the reaction conditions exert a significant influence on the optical properties of the CNDs prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to secured tags by UV microprinting

ce:section.ce:para: In order to directly mark products by generating patterns and coding information, we apply an upgraded UV microprinting protocol, inspired from our previous work [75], with CND based bioresists, (Fig. 4 .a). To speed up the fabrication of a reticulated poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) stamp, a mold featuring patterns of desired geometries and codes is first structured on silicon (Si) wafer substrate by maskless UV photolithography. The subsequent PDMS micromolding step is expedited in < 5 min by utilizing a hot embossing machine (see experimental section for details). A drop (0.25 mg) of CND based bio-resist, i.e., bio-nanocomposite, is directly deposited on the surface to be tagged without particular precautions nor cleaning and the PDMS stamp is then gently placed on top of it. Full UV insolation of the stack from the top transparent PDMS stamp allows to photopolymerize the bio-nanocomposite. The stamp is finally removed, leaving 3D photoluminescent bio-nanocomposite structures as tags on the surface. Such patterns will be referred as bio-tags thereafter in this work. The flexibility of the PDMS stamp enables the UV microprinting of a diverse range of surface configurations, vertical, horizontal, flat, and curved ones. Depending on the curvature of the target surface (if it is not flat), it may be necessary to apply an extra low mechanical pressure (a 100–200 N equivalent force) to the stamps for the first 10 s of UV exposure. Furthermore, this technique is not restricted to the dimensions of the tagged products. Nevertheless, the resolution of the marking is contingent upon the type of stamp utilized for its creation (Fig. 4 .b). When patterns of the stamp are groove type ones, their shapes are perfectly reproduced on the tag down to the minimal lateral resolution achieved with our photolithography equipment (3 µm). When patterns of the stamps are pit type ones, the minimum permissible marking resolution is around 6 µm. This resolution dissymmetry is in accordance with literature on UV nano-imprinting lithography [76–78].
The measured pattern thickness on the tag is 35 µm, which corresponds to the height of the PDMS pattern thickness, also matching the thickness of the mold patterns.

Fig. 4 .c illustrates a bio-tag in the shape of five squares marked on a glass slide. It is composed of a CNDs-E-based bio-nanocomposite and was observed under various light configurations: (1) bright field mode and no emission filter, (2) UV/blue excitation at 365 nm and no emission filter, (3) UV/blue excitation at 365 nm and emission filtered after 385 nm, and (4) UV/blue excitation at 381 nm and emission filtered after 520 nm. While the obtained bio-tag leaves a topographical print, the bio-nanocomposite is transparent to visible light (Fig. 4 .c (1)) and emits at the specific wavelengths of selected CNDs only under light excitation, following results on Fig. 3 .c. The bio-tag optical response could then be captured by optical microscopy or a smartphone camera; This optical signal contrast is mandatory for a regular app to uncode information coming from data matrix, QR code, etc. In the case of Fig. 4 .c (2), it is worth noting that the emission signal mainly comes from the bio-resist matrix itself which shows high natural photoluminescence under UV/blue excitation, largely screening the emission of CNDs. It presents a good enough optical signal contrast that can be easily detected. Nevertheless, the natural photoluminescence signal of the virgin bio-resist doesn't age well and decreases in time compared to the photoluminescence of CNDs. Reliable secured tags thus cannot be produced from virgin bio sourced resist itself, confirming that the presence of CNDs within the bio-resist is essential to offer a persistent optical signal of the tags. If now, the emission of bio-tags is filtered to only get the contribution of the CNDs, the final optical signal emanating from them can be tuned by playing with the emission wavelength for instance (cf. Fig. 4 .c (3) and (4)). Even if the CNDs exhibit a quite low PLQY, the overall optical bio-tag response is proportional to the pattern thickness [75] so making a bio-tag with a thickness of several tens of micrometers compensates it, inducing a stable contrast high enough to be detected by a smartphone camera.

As discussed earlier, the photostability of the fabricated bio-tags has to be confirmed under various environmental conditions to validate their concrete use on objects on an everyday basis. To test their robustness, ageing experiments were performed with aerospace small samples tagged with solvent free CND bio-nanocomposite based tags emitting at 484 nm and characterized with aerospace standards as objectives (resistance and adhesion for at least 30 years).

Following a ten-month period of exposure at room temperature, as illustrated in Figure S6.a, SI, no discernible changes were observed on the tag surface nor on the photoluminescence intensity. Adhesion and chemical tests following established procedures [79] were also performed regularly without noticeable effects. Besides, the bio-tags were subjected to temperatures of 50 °C, 90 °C and 120 °C for a period of 24 h, as illustrated in Figure S6.b, SI. It can be observed that an increase in temperature evidences a reduction of the residual thickness from 5 µm to 3 µm and a 30 % reduction in the PL intensity for the 120 °C case. However, the PL level and contrast are still good enough for bio-tags to be read. Again, adhesion and chemical resistance were not affected by temperature variations. Samples were further exposed for a period of three days at a temperature of 90 °C and a relative humidity of 65 % in a climatic chamber. While the PL signal decreased by 12 %, the high humidity content led to the partial detachment of the bio-nanocomposite tag from the surface (Figure S5.c, SI). To circumvent this lack of adhesion, we introduced an interpenetrating polymer network (IPN). The IPN is formed by the creation of a first layer of polymer network applied to the surface, consisting of a virgin OG 142 UV reticulable epoxy resist, crosslinked after 1 min of exposure with a UV lamp at 2000 mW/cm2 (λ = 365 nm). This polymer network is then swollen by the precursor of the second network, which is our photoluminescent bio-nanocomposite, before it is polymerized and cross-linked (Figure S7, SI). This technique enables the preparation of IPNs in which the entangled networks are formed using the same polymerization mechanism. Repeating the ageing process at 90 °C with a relative humidity of 65 % gave this time no detachment of tags.

Overall, this study demonstrates excellent mechanical and photostability for the proposed bio-nanocomposite based tags under a wide range of working conditions and comfort their use in concrete applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-counterfeiting, traceability and passport products applications
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, to illustrate the genericity of the marking technique, we produced secured bio-tags with a large range of tag designs encompassing logos, patterns, QR geocodes, bleam codes, alphanumeric codes, etc. For instance, the use of a secure QR geocode, which incorporates geolocation data to enhance the traceability of any given product and facilitate the generation of a digital passport, was made possible using a new traceability technology [3,80]. It enables the comprehensive and precise documentation of data within the object's digital passport. Once the data has been acquired and recorded in the QR geocode, it is guaranteed to be reliable, verifiable and secured. All pertinent data, including the identity of the individual, the time and date of occurrence, the nature of the event, and the location, has been stored in the tag. Similarly, the bleam code is a patented identification technology that functions in a manner analogous to that of a QR code [81]. The bleam code is a distinctive, durable, identification system with geolocalized functions which enables users to define all requisite parameters to generate a digital logbook for the tagged equipment, product, document. Bleam codes are highly resilient to damage and up to 100 bleams can be read simultaneously in milliseconds.

In view of demonstrating the effectiveness of our bio-nanocomposite-based tags in preventing counterfeiting and improving product traceability, bio-tags were integrated into an assortment of products. A variety of coding techniques were employed for the purpose of identifying a number of different items/ types of surfaces which are subject to lack of traceability and counterfeiting affairs: food, an airbag from a vehicle, an artistic piece, jewellery and aircraft components (Fig. 5 ).

Traceability of fruits and vegetables. Over the past decade, there has been a growing interest in food safety and the traceability of food from farm to fork. This is due to the increasing complexity of food supply chains, as well as recent food fraud scandals and food safety incidents, such as the increase in cases of mislabelling [82,83]. A CND-W based bio-tag in the shape of a “banana” bleam (Fig. 5 .a) was marked directly on a real fruit rough peel without any prior surface cleaning or treatment, confirming its good adhesion properties. It is worth noting that in indoor light conditions when UV photopolymerization is needed with a hight power UV torch, it may induce a local accelerated ripening. With the bleam being scanned by a smartphone using the UBLEAM app, the digital logbook of the fruit could be opened via an augmented reality interface and will allow a rigorous tracking in accordance with legal requirements for the fruit and vegetables traceability regulations [84].

Jewellery. Luxury products, which are often symbols of traditional craftsmanship, have long been a popular target for counterfeiters [85,86]. A CND-D-based bio-tag in the shape of a “Aéroprotec” bleam was applied to the surface of the jewel, as illustrated in Fig. 5 .b. As this marking is transparent to visible light, it does not affect the optical properties or general appearance of the stone. Through, the augmented reality interface, the end-owner gets information pertaining to the provenance of the stone, thereby providing with the assurance that he is purchasing or trading a truly timeless gemstone. The supporting video M1 illustrates how such a bleam tag is decoded by smartphone.

Aircraft components. In its long-term market forecast for the period 2019–2042, Airbus anticipates the delivery of 40,850 new passenger aircraft and freighters [87]. It is anticipated that this growth will result in a corresponding expansion of the global maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO) market [88]. This will provide an opportunity for those engaged in the illicit production of counterfeit products to supply a counterfeit item into the legitimate supply chain [89]. This is why the application of a secured external tag, which is both durable and invisible to the naked eye, can serve two distinct functions: (i) certifying the item in question, and (ii) containing further information regarding the manufacture and tracking of such item. Ageing tests described previously have demonstrated the required chemical and thermal resistance of CND based bio-tag, The aforementioned thermal resistance will enable the use of such tags any substrate subjected to temperature variations, including but not limited to aircraft engines, radiators, ovens, and so forth. As an example, a discarded aircraft blade was successfully tagged with a CND-E-based bio-tag in the curved bottom part. It represents a QR geocode linked to a Digital Passport Product (Fig. 5 .c) that can be verified (Video M2). It is worth noting that on reflective surface, in indoor conditions, the UV insolation time is reduced to a ten of seconds, most probably due to surface reflection which eases bulk photo-reticulation.

Artistic pieces. Various anti-counterfeiting approaches were developed to authenticate cellulose based work of art including hyperspectral imaging (HSI) [90]. Nevertheless, Zeng et al. demonstrated that this technique is not always sufficient to differentiate authentic paintings from counterfeits [91]. The fact that the proposed type of CND based bio-tagging is transparent to visible light and could be incorporate at the microscale should not impair artistic pieces and allow the authentication and tracking in a more effective manner. However, the porous nature of cellulose materials renders the bio-nanocomposite more readily absorbed. In such cases, IPN was implemented to improve the mechanical properties of our tags. Fig. 5 .d depicts the CNDs-E based bio tagging of a piece of paper featuring a printing reproduction of a design by the artist Victor Vasarely. Upon examination under an optical microscope in bright field mode, the zebra patterns became clearly visible. Under UV@365 nm excitation, emission of the CNDs-E can be detected along with the presence of entangled cellulose fibres whose positions can be used as PUF too. Note that this approach could be applied for secure packaging as well, made of cardboard for instance (see an example Figure S8, SI)

Vehicle spare parts. Bumpers, windscreen blades, various filters, clutches, paddles, suspensions, tyres, rear-view mirrors, lights, brake pads, brake discs, seat belts, and airbags are among the most often counterfeited parts and accessories in the automotive field [92]. Each of these items should be individually marked and tracked to ensure the quality and origin of the part. If not, it implies that counterfeits can enter the value supply chain in a number of ways [93]. Herein, a textured airbag casing in a car cockpit were successfully marked with a CNDs-D-based bio-nanocomposite in a shape of a logo associated to an alphanumeric code (Fig. 5 .e).

Coffee ground-based carbon nanodots dispersed in various solvents presenting different emission wavelengths were prepared by pyrolysis combined with solid/liquid extraction. This simple low-cost synthesis method offers several advantages, including a green synthesis path and a resource-efficient process with a relatively short reaction time (a few hours) compared to other techniques. It was demonstrated that the quantity of heteroatoms, such as oxygen or nitrogen, present in the CNDs had a significant impact on the emission spectrum. An increase in the quantity of heteroatoms will result in a red shift of the emission wavelength. A bio-nanocomposite was then formulated by mixing CNDs with a bio-sourced UV sensitive resist based on soya oil. It was finally deposited on a wide variety of substrates, including metal, cellulose, polymer, glass, and others and further patterned in a shape/code of interest by UV micro imprinting with lateral resolution down to few micrometers. Obtained bio-tags can be revealed and decoded via the photoluminescence emission of CNDs only when excited under UV/blue light. Ageing experiments proved the mechanical robustness, photostability, chemical resistance and good adhesion properties of fabricated tags and confirmed their compatibility with the tagging of concrete objects/documents in everyday uses. To tackle potential issues with porous substrates or lack of adhesion due to harsh environmental conditions (90 °C 65 % humidity) for instance, inter penetrating networks strategy was successfully introduced.

The proposed approach is versatile and be extended to any other photoluminescent colloidal system or powder, addressing any type of codes, pattern geometries scaling from millimeter to micrometer, on various substrate natures and curvatures. Such bio-tags were demonstrated to be alternative pertinent candidates for anti-counterfeiting, tracking and digital passport product solutions that we applied in user cases as examples.
